does: This paper investigates sentiment drift in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) applied to text summarization, finding that aligned models systematically shift summaries toward neutral sentiment compared to source documents. The authors conduct experiments across eight languages and multiple datasets to demonstrate this phenomenon is consistent and intensifies with stronger KL regularization. They introduce a Policy Attribution framework that decomposes the RLHF objective to identify which components drive sentiment suppression. The framework reveals that KL regularization is the primary culprit behind affective degradation. Building on these findings, they propose a sentiment-aware modification to the KL regularization term that mitigates drift while maintaining alignment quality.
connects: This work extends recent research on RLHF's effects on language model outputs, moving beyond fluency and factuality metrics to examine affective dimensions. It builds on sentiment analysis methods in NLP and connects to broader discourse on unintended consequences of alignment procedures, particularly how optimization for human preferences may suppress certain text properties. The paper responds to the growing recognition that standard metrics miss important aspects of summary quality, complementing work on sentiment preservation in neural summarization and multilingual robustness testing. It contributes to understanding the mechanisms within RLHF's objective functions—specifically KL divergence regularization—a topic increasingly relevant as these alignment techniques become standard for large language models.
enables: This work establishes sentiment preservation as a distinct evaluation criterion for summarization systems and opens pathways for developing affectively-aware alignment methods. It provides grounding for future research into other potentially overlooked properties affected by RLHF, including emotional nuance, narrative tone, and stylistic fidelity. The Policy Attribution framework offers a general tool for decomposing and understanding unintended effects from alignment objectives across different tasks and languages. The proposed sentiment-aware KL modification serves as a template for objective-level interventions that balance multiple desiderata in language model training. Results suggest that multilingual analysis of alignment phenomena could become standard practice, revealing cross-linguistic variations in how RLHF affects semantic and affective content.
